1→# COMP 421 Lab 2: The Yalnix Operating System Kernel
     2→
     3→## 1 Project Overview
     4→
     5→Implement an operating system kernel for the Yalnix operating system, running on the simulated RCS 421 computer system hardware. Your solution must be implemented in the C programming language (not C++ or other languages). The Yalnix operating system is similar in many ways to Unix, with multiple processes each having their own virtual address space. The system also supports a system console and a set of terminals for input and output.
     6→
     7→A single Linux process on CLEAR is made to behave like an RCS 421 computer to run your Yalnix kernel on. Separate X terminal windows simulate the terminals attached to the RCS 421 computer.
     8→
     9→All compiling, linking, and execution must be done on the CLEAR Linux system at Rice. The hardware simulation requires certain libraries and executable files that are only installed on CLEAR.
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## 2 The RCS 421 Computer System Hardware
    14→
    15→This section describes the hardware architecture of the Rice Computer Systems RCS 421 computer, a mythical computer architecture on which you will implement an operating system kernel in this project. The operating system, known as Yalnix, is described in Section 3. The RCS 421 computer system has the features of real hardware but is simplified somewhat to make the project more manageable.
    16→
    17→This section is written generally in the style of a real computer system hardware architecture manual. Everything in this section is something that a real operating system designer and implementer on such a computer would need to know about the hardware. Certain features of the operating system running on top of this hardware are mentioned here only as guidance in the implementation of any operating system for this hardware.
    18→
    19→### 2.1 Machine Registers
    20→
    21→This section defines the general purpose registers and privileged special registers of the RCS 421 computer system.
    22→
    23→#### 2.1.1 General Purpose Registers
    24→
    25→The RCS 421 computer has a number of general purpose registers that may be accessed from either user mode or kernel mode. These general purpose registers include the following:
    26→
    27→- **PC**, the program counter, contains the virtual address from which the currently executing instruction was fetched.
    28→- **SP**, the stack pointer, contains the virtual address of the top of the current process's stack.
    29→- **R0 through R7**, the eight general registers, used as accumulators or otherwise to hold temporary values during a computation.
    30→
    31→In reality, the real hardware has quite a few additional general purpose registers, but since you will be writing your operating system in C, not in machine language or assembly language, only these registers are relevant to the project.
    32→
    33→#### 2.1.2 Privileged Special Registers
    34→
    35→In addition to the general purpose CPU registers, the RCS 421 computer contains a number of privileged special registers, accessible only from kernel mode. Table 1 summarizes these privileged registers.
    36→
    37→**Table 1: RCS 421 Privileged Register Summary**
    38→
    39→| Name | Purpose | Readable | Details |
    40→|------|---------|----------|---------|
    41→| `REG_PTR0` | Page Table Register 0 | Yes | Section 2.2.4 |
    42→| `REG_PTR1` | Page Table Register 1 | Yes | Section 2.2.4 |
    43→| `REG_TLB_FLUSH` | TLB Flush Register | No | Section 2.2.5 |
    44→| `REG_VM_ENABLE` | Virtual Memory Enable Register | Yes | Section 2.2.6 |
    45→| `REG_VECTOR_BASE` | Vector Base Register | Yes | Section 2.4.1 |
    46→
    47→Details for the use of each of these registers are described in the following sections, as indicated in Table 1, where the operation of that component of the RCS 421 computer system hardware architecture is defined. Most of the privileged registers are both writable and readable, except for the `REG_TLB_FLUSH` register, which is write-only.
    48→
    49→All values written into or read from these privileged registers must be of type `RCS421RegVal`. This data type, along with other definitions for the simulated hardware, is defined in the file `comp421/hardware.h`. The `comp421/hardware.h` header file is located in the directory `/clear/courses/comp421/pub/include/comp421`. You can include the file `comp421/hardware.h` in your source file by using
    50→
    51→```c
    52→#include <comp421/hardware.h>
    53→```
    54→
    55→Do not copy this file into your own directory. The supplied Makefile template will enable the compiler to find this header file without it being located in your current directory.
    56→
    57→The RCS 421 hardware provides two privileged instructions for reading and writing these privileged machine registers:
    58→
    59→- **`void WriteRegister(int which, RCS421RegVal value)`** — Write `value` into the privileged machine register designated by `which`.
    60→
    61→- **`RCS421RegVal ReadRegister(int which)`** — Read the register specified by `which` and return its current value.
    62→
    63→Each privileged machine register is identified by a unique integer constant as noted in Table 1, passed as the `which` argument to the instructions. The file `comp421/hardware.h` defines the symbolic names for these constants.
    64→
    65→As noted above, the values of these registers are represented by the data type `RCS421RegVal`. You must use a C "cast" to convert other data types (such as addresses) to type `RCS421RegVal` when calling `WriteRegister`, and must also use a "cast" to convert the value returned by `ReadRegister` to the desired type if you need to interpret the returned value as any type other than an `RCS421RegVal`.
    66→
    67→### 2.2 Memory Subsystem
    68→
    69→#### 2.2.1 Overview
    70→
    71→The memory subsystem of the RCS 421 computer supports physical memory size that depends on the amount of hardware memory installed. The physical memory is divided into pages of size `PAGESIZE` bytes.
    72→
    73→The individual pages of this physical memory may be mapped into the address space of a running process through virtual memory supported by the RCS 421 hardware and initialized and controlled by your Yalnix operating system kernel. The hardware component that implements the hardware control for these virtual memory mappings is known as the Memory Management Unit, or MMU. As with the physical memory, the virtual memory is divided into pages of size `PAGESIZE`, and any page of the virtual address space may be mapped to any page of physical memory through the page table of each running process.
    74→
    75→The constant `PAGESIZE` is defined in `comp421/hardware.h`. In addition, this header file also defines a number of other constants and macros to make dealing with addresses and page numbers easier:
    76→
    77→- **`PAGESIZE`**: The size in bytes of each physical memory and virtual memory page.
    78→
    79→- **`PAGEOFFSET`**: A bit mask that can be used to extract the byte offset within a page, given an arbitrary physical or virtual address. For an address `addr`, the value `(addr & PAGEOFFSET)` is the byte offset within the page where `addr` points.
    80→
    81→- **`PAGEMASK`**: A bit mask that can be used to extract the beginning address of a page, given an arbitrary physical or virtual address. For example, for an address `addr`, the value `(addr & PAGEMASK)` is the beginning address of the page where `addr` points.
    82→
    83→- **`PAGESHIFT`**: The log base 2 of `PAGESIZE`, which is thus also the number of bits in the offset in a physical or virtual address. For example, for any page number (virtual or physical) `num`, then `(num << PAGESHIFT)` is the address (respectively, virtual or physical) of the beginning of that page. Or, in reverse, if `addr` is the address (virtual or physical) of the beginning of a page, then `(addr >> PAGESHIFT)` is the page number (respectively, virtual or physical) of that page. For any value `x`, then `(x << PAGESHIFT)` is the same as `(x * PAGESIZE)`, and `(x >> PAGESHIFT)` is the same as `(x / PAGESIZE)`.
    84→
    85→- **`UP_TO_PAGE(addr)`**: A C preprocessor macro that rounds address `addr` up to the next highest page boundary. If `addr` is already on a page boundary, it returns `addr`.
    86→
    87→- **`DOWN_TO_PAGE(addr)`**: A C preprocessor macro that rounds address `addr` down to the next lowest page boundary. If `addr` is already on a page boundary, it returns `addr`.
    88→
    89→**NOTE:** Due to RCS 421 hardware restrictions (in reality, due to restrictions in the underlying Linux operating system), the bottom (lowest-most addressed) `MEM_INVALID_SIZE` bytes (equivalently, the lowest-most `MEM_INVALID_PAGES` pages) of the address space are inaccessible; specifically, any address in this range cannot be used as an address, as the hardware disallows such addresses. Before virtual memory has been enabled, this means that any physical address in this range of addresses will cause an error. After virtual memory has been enabled, this means that all page table entries (`MEM_INVALID_PAGES` page table entries) corresponding to this address range must all have a valid bit of 0. By enforcing this restriction, the hardware attempts to ensure that accidental accesses to memory using a NULL pointer are detectable.
    90→
    91→Note that the first `MEM_INVALID_PAGES` pages of physical memory do really exist and can be used the same as any other page of physical memory, once virtual memory has been enabled. That is, a valid virtual page can be mapped to any page of physical memory, including a physical page with a physical page number less than `MEM_INVALID_PAGES`, since in then accessing such a page, you would be using a virtual address, not directly a physical address, to access the page; as long as the virtual address you
... (truncated 1147 chars)

     2→
     3→>>>> You MUST edit each place marked by ">>>>" below to replace

    15→
    16→/*
    17→ *  Load a program into the current process's address space.  The
    18→ *  program comes from the Unix file identified by "name", and its
    19→ *  arguments come from the array at "args", which is in standard
    20→ *  argv format.
    21→ *
    22→ *  Returns:
    23→ *      0 on success
    24→ *     -1 on any error for which the current process is still runnable
    25→ *     -2 on any error for which the current process is no longer runnable
    26→ *
    27→ *  This function, after a series of initial checks, deletes the
    28→ *  contents of Region 0, thus making the current process no longer
    29→ *  runnable.  Before this point, it is possible to return ERROR
    30→ *  to an Exec() call that has called LoadProgram, and this function
    31→ *  returns -1 for errors up to this point.  After this point, the
    32→ *  contents of Region 0 no longer exist, so the calling user process
    33→ *  is no longer runnable, and this function returns -2 for errors
    34→ *  in this case.
    35→ */
    36→int
    37→LoadProgram(char *name, char **args)
    38→{
    39→    int fd;
    40→    int status;
    41→    struct loadinfo li;
    42→    char *cp;
    43→    char *cp2;
    44→    char **cpp;
    45→    char *argbuf;
    46→    int i;
    47→    unsigned long argcount;
    48→    int size;
    49→    int text_npg;
    50→    int data_bss_npg;
    51→    int stack_npg;
    52→
    53→    TracePrintf(0, "LoadProgram '%s', args %p\n", name, args);
    54→
    55→    if ((fd = open(name, O_RDONLY)) < 0) {
    56→	TracePrintf(0, "LoadProgram: can't open file '%s'\n", name);
    57→	return (-1);
    58→    }
    59→
    60→    status = LoadInfo(fd, &li);
    61→    TracePrintf(0, "LoadProgram: LoadInfo status %d\n", status);
    62→    switch (status) {
    63→	case LI_SUCCESS:
    64→	    break;
    65→	case LI_FORMAT_ERROR:
    66→	    TracePrintf(0,
    67→		"LoadProgram: '%s' not in Yalnix format\n", name);
    68→	    close(fd);
    69→	    return (-1);
    70→	case LI_OTHER_ERROR:
    71→	    TracePrintf(0, "LoadProgram: '%s' other error\n", name);
    72→	    close(fd);
    73→	    return (-1);
    74→	default:
    75→	    TracePrintf(0, "LoadProgram: '%s' unknown error\n", name);
    76→	    close(fd);
    77→	    return (-1);
    78→    }
    79→    TracePrintf(0, "text_size 0x%lx, data_size 0x%lx, bss_size 0x%lx\n",
    80→	li.text_size, li.data_size, li.bss_size);
    81→    TracePrintf(0, "entry 0x%lx\n", li.entry);
    82→
    83→    /*
    84→     *  Figure out how many bytes are needed to hold the arguments on
    85→     *  the new stack that we are building.  Also count the number of
    86→     *  arguments, to become the argc that the new "main" gets called with.
    87→     */
    88→    size = 0;
    89→    for (i = 0; args[i] != NULL; i++) {
    90→	size += strlen(args[i]) + 1;
    91→    }
    92→    argcount = i;
    93→    TracePrintf(0, "LoadProgram: size %d, argcount %d\n", size, argcount);
    94→
    95→    /*
    96→     *  Now save the arguments in a separate buffer in Region 1, since
    97→     *  we are about to delete all of Region 0.
    98→     */
    99→    cp = argbuf = (char *)malloc(size);
   100→    for (i = 0; args[i] != NULL; i++) {
   101→	strcpy(cp, args[i]);
   102→	cp += strlen(cp) + 1;
   103→    }
   104→
   105→    /*
   106→     *  The arguments will get copied starting at "cp" as set below,
   107→     *  and the argv pointers to the arguments (and the argc value)
   108→     *  will get built starting at "cpp" as set below.  The value for
   109→     *  "cpp" is computed by subtracting off space for the number of
   110→     *  arguments plus 4 (for the argc value, a 0 (AT_NULL) to
   111→     *  terminate the auxiliary vector, a NULL pointer terminating

   114→     *  value must also be aligned down to a multiple of 8 boundary.
   115→     */

1→#ifndef	_loadinfo_h
     2→#define _loadinfo_h
     3→
     4→#include <sys/types.h>
     5→
     6→/*
     7→ *  Define the external interface to the LoadInfo() function.
     8→ *
     9→ *  LoadInfo() is passed an open file descriptor number and a pointer
    10→ *  to a "struct loadinfo".  The file descriptor should be open
    11→ *  to an ELF format executable file that has been linked to
    12→ *  execute as a Yalnix user process.  On successful return,
    13→ *  LoadInfo will have filled in the struct loadinfo with the
    14→ *  information needed by LoadProgram() to load the Yalnix user
    15→ *  program into memory and set it up for execution.
    16→ *
    17→ *  LoadInfo() returns 0 (= LI_SUCCESS) on success.  Any other
    18→ *  return value indicates an error, as defined by the other LI_*
    19→ *  values below.
    20→ */
    21→
    22→struct loadinfo {
    23→    size_t text_size;	/* size of text */
    24→    size_t data_size;	/* size of data */
    25→    size_t bss_size;	/* size of bss */
    26→    void *entry;	/* entry point of executable */
    27→};
    28→
    29→extern int LoadInfo(int, struct loadinfo *);
    30→
    31→#define LI_SUCCESS	0
    32→#define LI_FORMAT_ERROR	1	/* not linked as a Yalnix user program */
    33→#define LI_OTHER_ERROR	2
    34→
    35→#endif /*!_loadinfo_h*/
    36→

1. KernelStart - kernel initialization
2. SetKernelBrk - kernel heap management
3. Trap handlers for all interrupt types
4. System calls: Fork, Exec, Exit, Wait, GetPid, Brk, Delay, TtyRead, TtyWrite
5. Context switching
6. Virtual memory management
7. LoadProgram function

Let me read the sample test programs to understand what output is expected.

Implement TtyRead and TtyWrite syscalls

1→"""
     2→COMP 421 Lab 2 (Yalnix Kernel) Test Definitions
     3→
     4→Test definitions map test program names to expected output patterns.
     5→The runner executes each test under the Yalnix simulator and checks output.
     6→
     7→Unlike Lab 3 (where TTYLOG.0 is the FS server and TTYLOG.1 is the test
     8→program), Lab 2 output comes from a combination of:
     9→  - Yalnix stdout: printf/fprintf output from test programs
    10→  - TRACE file: TracePrintf output from kernel and user (enabled with -lk 0 -lu 0)
    11→  - TTYLOG files: TtyWrite/TtyPrintf output (often empty)
    12→
    13→The runner combines all three sources before passing to check functions.
    14→Each check function takes (output,) and returns (bool, str).
    15→
    16→25 tests, 100 points total.
    17→"""
    18→
    19→from typing import Tuple
    20→
    21→# Programs that are compiled but not scored — they're targets for Exec tests.
    22→EXTRA_PROGRAMS = ["exec_target", "list_args"]
    23→
    24→
    25→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    26→# Boot & Init (3 tests, 10 points)
    27→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    28→
    29→def check_boot(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    30→    """init_exit: Prints 'Entering init_exit' and 'Exiting' via printf."""
    31→    if "Entering init_exit" not in output:
    32→        return (False, "Missing 'Entering init_exit'")
    33→    if "Exiting" not in output:
    34→        return (False, "Missing 'Exiting'")
    35→    return (True, "")
    36→
    37→
    38→def check_getpid(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    39→    """init_getpid: Prints 'init: pid:' via printf."""
    40→    if "init: pid:" not in output:
    41→        return (False, "Missing 'init: pid:' — GetPid may not work")
    42→    return (True, "")
    43→
    44→
    45→def check_print(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    46→    """init_print: Prints 'hello world' via printf."""
    47→    if "hello world" not in output:
    48→        return (False, "Missing 'hello world'")
    49→    return (True, "")
    50→
    51→
    52→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    53→# Fork (4 tests, 17 points)
    54→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    55→
    56→def check_fork_basic(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    57→    """fork_basic: TracePrintf prints 'I'm the parent' and 'I'm the child'."""
    58→    if "I'm the parent" not in output and "parent" not in output.lower():
    59→        return (False, "Missing parent message")
    60→    if "I'm the child" not in output and "child" not in output.lower():
    61→        return (False, "Missing child message")
    62→    return (True, "")
    63→
    64→
    65→def check_fork_print(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    66→    """fork_print: Prints 'FORK2>' lines and 'FORK2 done.' via printf."""
    67→    if "FORK2>" not in output:
    68→        return (False, "Missing 'FORK2>' output lines")
    69→    if "FORK2 done." not in output:
    70→        return (False, "Missing 'FORK2 done.' — fork/recursion may have failed")
    71→    return (True, "")
    72→
    73→
    74→def check_fork_recurse(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    75→    """fork_recurse: Prints 'CHILD' and 'PARENT' messages, uses Delay."""
    76→    if "CHILD" not in output:
    77→        return (False, "Missing 'CHILD' message")
    78→    if "PARENT" not in output:
    79→        return (False, "Missing 'PARENT' message")
    80→    return (True, "")
    81→
    82→
    83→def check_fork_multi(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    84→    """fork_multi: TracePrintf prints 'done, forked' for each process."""
    85→    if "done, forked" not in output:
    86→        return (False, "Missing 'done, forked' — fork loop may have failed")
    87→    return (True, "")
    88→
    89→
    90→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    91→# Wait & Exit (5 tests, 23 points)
    92→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    93→
    94→def check_wait_status(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    95→    """wait_status: TracePrintf prints 'exited with status: 15'."""
    96→    if "exited with status: 15" not in output:
    97→        return (False, "Missing 'exited with status: 15' — Wait/Exit status may be wrong")
    98→    return (True, "")
    99→
   100→
   101→def check_wait_zombie(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
   102→    """wait_zombie: Prints 'FORKWAIT> GOOD!' via printf."""
   103→    if "FORKWAIT> GOOD!" not in output:
   104→        return (False, "Missing 'FORKWAIT> GOOD!' — zombie collection failed")
   105→    return (True, "")
   106→
   107→
   108→def check_wait_before_exit(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
   109→    """wait_before_exit: Parent waits, child delays then exits with 123."""
   110→    if "wait returned" not in output:
   111→        return (False, "Missing 'wait returned' — Wait may not have returned")
   112→    if "123" not in output:
   113→        return (False, "Missing status 123 in wait output")
   114→    return (True, "")
   115→
   116→
   117→def check_wait_after_exit(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
   118→    """wait_after_exit: Child exits immediately with 123, parent delays then waits."""
   119→    if "wait returned" not in output:
   120→        return (False, "Missing 'wait returned' — Wait may not have returned")
   121→    if "123" not in output:
   122→        return (False, "Missing status 123 in wait output")
   123→    return (True, "")
   124→
   125→
   126→def check_wait_loop(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
   127→    """wait_loop: 10 iterations of fork-wait, prints 'FORKWAIT' and 'GOOD!' each time."""
   128→    good_count = output.count("GOOD!")
   129→    if good_count < 5:
   130→        return (False, f"Expected multiple 'GOOD!' entries, found {good_count}")
   131→    return (True, "")
   132→
   133→
   134→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   135→# Exec (2 tests, 10 points)
   136→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   137→
   138→def check_exec(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
   139→    """exec_wrapper: Calls Exec('exec_target',...) which prints 'argv[0]:' via TracePrintf."""
   140→    if "argv[" not in output and "Calling exec" not in output:
   141→        return (False, "Missing exec target output — Exec may have failed")
   142→    if "Calling exec" in output and "argv[" not in output:
   143→        return (False, "Exec was called but target didn't execute — Exec failed")
   144→    return (True, "")
   145→
   146→
   147→def check_fork_exec(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
   148→    """fork_exec: Forks then both parent and child Exec('list_args',...).
   149→    list_args prints 'arguments passed in' via both printf and TracePrintf."""
   150→    if "arguments passed in" not in output:
   151→        if "Exec failed" in output or "Fork failed" in output:
   152→            return (False, "Exec or Fork reported failure")
   153→        return (False, "Missing 'arguments passed in' — Exec to list_args may have failed")
   154→    return (True, "")
   155→
   156→
   157→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   158→# Memory (5 tests, 20 points)
   159→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   160→
   161→def check_brk(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:

22/25 tests pass (89%). The only failures are the 3 TTY write tests. The issue is that TtyWrite is timing out - the process calls TtyTransmit but the TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT handler isn't properly waking the process. The problem is likely that when there's only one process and it blocks on TtyWrite, we schedule to idle, but when the transmit interrupt comes we need to properly handle returning from idle to the unblocked process.

Let me look at the issue. The `ttywrite` test does TtyWrite then Exit. After TtyTransmit, the process blocks and we schedule idle. The transmit interrupt fires, puts the process on ready queue, but then when the clock fires and idle gets preempted, the process should run. The issue might be that the TTY write buffer handling is problematic - I'm using `tty_xmit_buf` as a single shared buffer per terminal, but when queuing writes, each writer needs its own copy.

Let me fix the TtyWrite implementation.

The TTY write issue is that the process blocks, we switch to idle, and when the transmit interrupt arrives, the process gets put on the ready queue. But the output shows "exit code: 124" which is the timeout signal (124 is the exit code for `timeout` command). This means the kernel is hanging.

Let me look more carefully at the TTY write flow. The issue might be with how I handle the transmit buffer per terminal - I use a single shared buffer. When the first process starts a write, the data is in `tty_xmit_buf[tty_id]`. When the transmit completes and there's a queued writer, I try to re-transmit from the same `tty_xmit_buf[tty_id]`, but the queued writer's data isn't there yet.


The real problem is that when the transmit handler tries to start the next write, it's pulling from the buffer that was filled by the first writer, not the queued one - the queued writer never got a chance to copy its data into the buffer in the first place.

Looking at the sequence more carefully: when we queue a write instead of executing it immediately, we're storing the length in `delay_ticks` but the actual data stays in the shared `tty_xmit_buf`, which means a queued writer's data overwrites whatever the active writer put there. But the timeout hanging the kernel suggests something deeper - when the transmit interrupt fires and clears the active writer, we're not properly transitioning to the next queued writer, so the system gets stuck waiting for something that never happens. Looking at the test output more carefully - the TTYLOG files are completely empty even though TtyWrite is being called, which means the transmit interrupt is firing and unblocking the process, but the actual output isn't making it to the log. Then we return to `kern_ttywrite`, which returns `len`. Then we go back to `trap_kernel`, which sets `info->regs[0] = rc`. Then the handler returns and the user process gets `len` as return value.

Same results. The TTY tests still fail with exit code 124 (timeout). The idle process Pause loop in kernel mode is not receiving interrupts, as I suspected. The "kernel is not interruptable" means Pause() in kernel mode doesn't deliver interrupts.

I need to make idle a proper user-mode process. Let me think about how to do this.

Since I can't modify the Makefile or add additional files, I need to create the idle process entirely from within the kernel. The approach:

1. Allocate code page and stack page in Region 0 for idle
2. Write machine code for the idle loop into the code page


3. Set up the idle process's ExceptionInfo with the program counter, stack pointer, and PSR set to user mode
4. Save idle's initial context so it can be restored later, then return from KernelStart with init's context on the first boot

The key insight is that idle needs to run the Pause loop in user mode so interrupts work properly. I'll create a DoIdle function in the kernel that just loops...

Actually, I realize the issue: user programs get their own copy of Pause() linked into their Region 0 address space from the hardware library, so idle can call Pause() directly without needing to access kernel text.

But since I'm creating the idle process in-kernel without a compiled binary, I'd need to either compile an idle binary or write raw machine code for the Pause() instruction—and I don't know what that machine code is.

Let me try a different approach entirely: skip the separate idle process and just have the scheduler itself wait for interrupts when no processes are ready.

Exploring the scheduler loop...

The real issue is that we need *some* process running in user mode to receive interrupts, and that's what idle is for—even a simple infinite loop would work since hardware interrupts will still fire and wake up blocked processes when their I/O completes. interrupt will also fire. The scheduler will then switch to the unblocked process.

So I can make idle's code just: `loop: jmp loop` — which is the x86-64 instruction `0xEB 0xFE`.

Let me try this approach:
1. In KernelStart, allocate a code page and stack page for idle in Region 0
2. Write `0xEB 0xFE` (infinite loop) into the code page
3. Set up idle's initial context with pc pointing to the loop and sp pointing to the stack

For step 3, the tricky part is setting up the initial SavedContext. When we ContextSwitch to idle for the first time, it needs to "return" from ContextSwitch into a state where the hardware will pop an ExceptionInfo from idle's kernel stack and jump to user-mode idle code.

I think I should set up idle similarly to init — create its process control block, allocate pages in its region, write the infinite loop code, and manually construct the ExceptionInfo on its kernel stack. The key insight is that I can use the same initialization trick as init: when idle is first scheduled, I'll set up the context so that ContextSwitch can properly restore into user mode without needing a prior saved state. idle_proc
- Switch to idle's Region 0
- Set up info (the KernelStart ExceptionInfo) for idle
- Return from KernelStart (starts idle in user mode)

But then I can't also start init! The solution:
- First, create idle_proc and save its context
- Then create init_proc
- Return from KernelStart as init (not idle)
- When idle is needed, ContextSwitch to idle

The problem is saving idle's initial context. I need idle to have a context that, when restored, results in it running in user mode.

Hmm, let me try yet another approach. What if I use ContextSwitch to COPY the init process context for idle? Like:

1. Create idle process, set up its Region 0 with code+stack
2. Use ContextSwitch similar to Fork to save idle's initial kernel state

Actually, here's a cleaner solution. Instead of trying to save idle's context in KernelStart, I'll set up idle's ExceptionInfo directly with user-mode code and stack, make it the current process, then return from KernelStart to start it running. Init gets created and queued separately, and on the first clock interrupt, I'll switch to init instead. This way KernelStart returns as idle, but the scheduler immediately picks init on the next interrupt. user mode
- First clock interrupt fires, scheduler sees init on ready queue, switches to init
- Init begins running

For this to work, I need to make init's initial ExceptionInfo available when it's first scheduled. The ExceptionInfo for init needs to be on init's kernel stack.

When we ContextSwitch to init for the first time:
- ContextSwitch restores init's SavedContext
- ContextSwitch returns... but init hasn't been ContextSwitched before!

This is the bootstrapping problem. init has never been run, so it has no SavedContext.

What if I use a similar approach to Fork? In Fork, I save the parent's context in the child. For the init process bootstrap:

1. During KernelStart, I ContextSwitch to save idle's context
2. Then I ContextSwitch to "create" init's context by copying the current state

The key insight is that when init is switched to, it needs to "return" from ContextSwitch in a state where it can set up its ExceptionInfo and return to user mode. This is what the init process loading already does.

Looking at my current approach: idle gets created and its context is saved, then init is created with LoadProgram setting up its info, current_proc becomes init_proc, and KernelStart returns to start init in user mode. When init later blocks and we switch to idle, the problem is that idle resumes in kernel mode instead of where it should be.

I'm thinking about using a fake ExceptionInfo on idle's kernel stack that the hardware can use to return to user mode, similar to how trap handlers work. I could write a small function that constructs this ExceptionInfo on the stack...

Now I'm re-reading the ContextSwitch documentation to understand how the CPU state gets saved and restored. It looks like ContextSwitch saves the current CPU state in a SavedContext structure, then MySwitchFunc handles switching the page tables between processes, and when MySwitchFunc returns, the hardware restores the saved state.

When ContextSwitch restores idle's state, it restores the PC to a point in KernelStart (right after the ContextSwitch call) and the SP to idle's kernel stack. So when we return from KernelStart running as init, the hardware is using init's page table (set during LoadProgram), which means init's kernel stack is active. Later when we switch to idle via MySwitchFunc, we load idle's page table and flush the TLB, which brings idle's kernel stack into view—but that kernel stack still contains the stack frames and local variables from when KernelStart was running. So this approach only works if the idle loop doesn't call any kernel functions—just a simple infinite loop with no external calls.

I can define a minimal DoIdle function that's just an infinite loop, which compiles down to a single jump instruction without any address references. Then I'll map that physical page into the idle process's user-accessible region so it can execute from user mode without triggering memory traps.

The plan is to locate the DoIdle function in kernel memory, find its physical page, and map it into idle's address space at a user-accessible address.

But I'm running into the bootstrapping issue again—when idle first gets scheduled, it needs a SavedContext to resume from, but if I return from KernelStart as idle directly, it starts executing in user mode right away. The first interrupt will save its state, but I need to think through how the context switching actually works when idle gets preempted later. I see the bootstrapping problem extends to init as well. If both idle and init start from KernelStart, then when init eventually blocks and needs to switch to idle, idle still won't have a SavedContext. The hint in the instructions suggests using ContextSwitch itself to initialize contexts for processes that haven't run yet—essentially using it to set up the SavedContext without actually switching processes. does this. The issue is what happens when this context is later restored. Let me trace through the idle process flow: in KernelStart, I set up idle's user-mode pages and call ContextSwitch to capture its initial state without actually switching. Later when idle gets scheduled, ContextSwitch restores that saved context and returns us back to the point in KernelStart where we captured it.

The problem is getting from kernel mode back to user mode when idle resumes. The hardware normally handles this by popping the ExceptionInfo when KernelStart returns, but KernelStart already returned for init. I could modify the ExceptionInfo that was originally pushed to point to idle's entry instead, letting the hardware restore user-mode execution when that same return happens. goes when KernelStart returns. When KernelStart returns, the hardware pops this ExceptionInfo and goes to user mode.

Each process's kernel stack is separate. When idle was created, its kernel stack was set up with an ExceptionInfo from the initial boot. Wait, no — the initial ExceptionInfo is on the boot kernel stack, which IS idle's kernel stack (since idle uses the boot page table).

Hmm wait, is the initial ExceptionInfo still on idle's kernel stack? Let's see:
- KernelStart is called with ExceptionInfo on the boot kernel stack
- We save idle's context via ContextSwitch (idle uses the boot kernel stack)
- We create init, switch to init's Region 0
- Modify info (which points to the ExceptionInfo on the boot/idle kernel stack... wait, no)

Actually, `info` is a pointer to the ExceptionInfo on idle's kernel stack. After creating init and switching to init's Region 0, `info` still points to the same physical memory, but now that memory isn't mapped in init's page table — init has its own kernel stack mapped at those virtual addresses instead.

So when LoadProgram modifies `info`, it's actually modifying init's kernel stack, not idle's. That's the issue.

Maybe I should reconsider the whole approach. What if idle isn't a special case at all?

Instead, when there's nothing to run, I call ContextSwitch to save the current state and transition into idle. When idle's context gets restored later, it returns from that ContextSwitch call, still inside the handler. Then we return from the handler, and the hardware pops the ExceptionInfo to resume whoever was running before the interrupt.

But that's the problem—we'd be resuming the blocked process, which shouldn't happen. Unless I modify the ExceptionInfo to point to idle user-mode code before returning. That could actually work.

When there's no ready process, instead of ContextSwitching to idle, I modify the current ExceptionInfo to point to idle code, return from the handler, and the hardware restores that modified state to start idle in user mode. When the next interrupt fires, the handler sees idle's ExceptionInfo and can schedule the unblocked process normally.

The blocked process's state was already saved by ContextSwitch when it was originally scheduled out, so when it gets unblocked and scheduled again, ContextSwitch restores its context and the handler returns with its original ExceptionInfo intact.

But now I'm realizing the issue: if I don't ContextSwitch to idle, I'm not saving the blocked process's ExceptionInfo anywhere before I overwrite it. I need to save it first before modifying the ExceptionInfo to point to idle code.

Actually, the key insight is that the ExceptionInfo sitting on the kernel stack already IS the saved context—I just need to preserve it in the PCB, then modify the ExceptionInfo in place to set up idle's execution state (program counter, stack pointer, processor status register), update current_proc to point to idle, and return from the handler. This way idle runs in user mode and can receive interrupts properly.

But there's a subtle problem: once I switch Region 0 to idle's page table, the blocked process's kernel stack becomes inaccessible, so I can't modify its ExceptionInfo anymore. I need to save the context to the PCB *before* switching the page table, then set up idle's ExceptionInfo on idle's own kernel stack instead.

Let me try a different approach: I'll write the idle loop machine code directly into a page, set up idle's kernel stack with a fake ExceptionInfo manually, and use ContextSwitch to jump into it. This way idle runs in user mode like any other process.

Setting up idle_proc...

Now I'm realizing the issue with returning from the handler—there's no actual call stack to return through since idle never went through a normal handler invocation. I need to think through what actually happens when the hardware triggers a trap and the handler returns.

When a trap fires, the hardware pushes the ExceptionInfo onto the kernel stack and calls the handler. When the handler returns, it pops its own stack frame, leaving the ExceptionInfo at the top of the kernel stack, which the hardware then pops to restore user state and switch back to user mode.

For idle, if I can set up its kernel stack with just an ExceptionInfo positioned correctly, and arrange its SavedContext so it appears to be at the point right before returning from a handler, then the hardware would pop that ExceptionInfo and transition to user mode. The problem is I can't directly control the SavedContext format since it's opaque.

Let me try a different approach using ContextSwitch more creatively—I'll set up idle with a user-mode code page, switch to its Region 0, and then modify...

Continuing with the setup...

when idle resumes from the ContextSwitch, we're back in KernelStart with idle's Region 0 and kernel stack active. The ExceptionInfo I set up on idle's kernel stack gets popped when we return, which transitions idle into user mode at the right address. The key is that by the time we return from KernelStart, all the memory regions and stacks are already configured for idle to run independently. modify `info` which was a pointer passed to KernelStart. The pointer value is a virtual address pointing to the ExceptionInfo on the boot kernel stack. After switching to idle's Region 0 (step 2), the boot kernel stack IS idle's kernel stack (if idle uses the boot pt0). So `info` still points to the right place.

But after step 4 (ContextSwitch), when idle is later resumed:
- idle's context is restored
- We're on idle's kernel stack
- `info` was a local variable pointing to the ExceptionInfo on the kernel stack
- But `info` was saved in idle's SavedContext... actually, info is a parameter to KernelStart. It's on the kernel stack as part of KernelStart's frame. When ContextSwitch restores idle's context, the stack pointer is restored, and `info` should be accessible.

Now I need to verify the ExceptionInfo is positioned correctly. When KernelStart was initially called, the hardware pushed ExceptionInfo onto the kernel stack, and `info` points to that location. When we return from KernelStart, the hardware pops it back off. Once idle is resumed and SavedContext is restored, the stack pointer goes back to where it was in KernelStart, so `info` still references the right ExceptionInfo on the stack, and when we return from KernelStart the hardware pops it so idle can run in user mode. But I need to trace through what happens when idle was first set up versus when init was set up in the later steps. The virtual address of ExceptionInfo on the kernel stack is fixed near KERNEL_STACK_LIMIT, and since both idle and init have their kernel stacks mapped to the same virtual addresses, the `info` pointer references the same virtual address but different physical pages depending on which Region 0 is active. So when Region 0 switches between idle and init, `info` still points to the same virtual address, but it's accessing different physical memory—idle's ExceptionInfo in step 3 versus init's in step 8. The confusion is that `info` is a parameter stored on the kernel stack itself, so its value (the virtual address) remains constant even as the underlying physical memory it points to changes with each Region 0 switch.

Now I'm verifying the return path: when we return from KernelStart in step 9, the current Region 0 is init's, so the hardware pops the ExceptionInfo from init's kernel stack, which is correct.

Later in step 5 when idle resumes, Region 0 switches back to idle's, and the hardware pops the ExceptionInfo that was set up in step 3, also correct.

Now I need to implement the idle user-mode code—a simple infinite loop in x86-64 machine code that I'll place in a physical frame mapped into idle's Region 0 at a user-accessible virtual address.

Setting up the code page... Setting up the idle process's stack page with the same allocation and permission setup, then switching to its page table and flushing the TLB before writing the idle loop code into memory. Finishing the register initialization, then setting up the idle process's memory layout with its break point and stack base. I'm saving the idle context and performing a context switch to initialize it, with a guard to ensure we only do this setup once before returning to enter user-mode idle. Now I'm ready to create the init process.

The TTY tests still time out. The problem is that idle runs in kernel mode (Pause loop), and the kernel is not interruptable, so no interrupts arrive. I need to make idle a proper user-mode process. I'll write an infinite loop in x86-64 machine code into a Region 0 page for idle.

blocks.text:
The TTY write tests are still failing with timeouts. The TTYLOG files are empty, meaning no output is being written to the terminal. Let me look at the TRACE output more carefully:

For test_ttywrite:
```
TLB before read()
Doing read: 0x10000 0x7130
Bottom of KernelStart:
YalnixTtyWrite: pid 1
Bottom of 
```

So the TtyWrite syscall is being called, but the output never appears in the TTYLOG. This means the TtyTransmit is either not being called, or the TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT interrupt is never being delivered (so the process stays blocked forever).


The idle process might not be working correctly in user mode, or the x86-64 machine code approach has issues with the simulator. Looking at the trace more carefully, KernelStart is completing and the TtyWrite handler is being invoked, but then execution stalls—likely because the process blocks waiting for the TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT interrupt that never arrives.

Since the Yalnix simulator runs on x86-64 Linux machines, the architecture should support that instruction, but maybe the issue is elsewhere. I need to trace through how the idle process gets scheduled, how TtyWrite blocks the process, and whether TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT is actually being delivered to wake it up.
Still 22/25 - the TTY write tests are still timing out. The idle process in user mode isn't working as expected. Let me examine the current code to debug the issue.

OK, so the flow for TTY write should work like this:

1. Init calls TtyWrite → kern_ttywrite is called
2. Terminal is free → memcpy to tty_xmit_buf, set active, call TtyTransmit, block init, schedule
3. schedule finds no ready processes → switch to idle
4. Idle enters user mode (infinite loop)
5. Hardware delivers TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT (after transmit completes)
6. trap_tty_transmit unblocks init (enqueue_ready)
7. trap_tty_transmit returns to idle (user mode)


8. Next clock tick → trap_clock → idle is running, ready_head has init → schedule init
9. Init resumes from schedule → kern_ttywrite returns len

So there are actually five context switches happening: init to idle, the TTY transmit trap, back to idle's user mode, the clock trap, and finally idle to init. But I'm realizing there's something subtle going on between the transmit trap and when init actually gets scheduled.

When the TTY transmit interrupt fires while idle is running, the trap handler executes on idle's kernel stack, enqueues init to the ready queue, and returns back to idle's user mode. Then the clock interrupt fires on idle's kernel stack, sees that ready_head is no longer null, and calls schedule to switch to init. But here's the thing—the TTY transmit might not even fire before the test times out, or it could fire right alongside the clock interrupt. Either way, I need to figure out why the process is timing out in the first place. Let me trace through what happens when idle resumes via MySwitchFunc and KernelStart returns.

The hardware is supposed to pop the ExceptionInfo and transition to user mode, but that mechanism depends on how the Yalnix simulator handles returns from trap handlers. When idle gets resumed through ContextSwitch, it's not returning from a trap in the normal sense—it's just a regular C function return from ContextSwitch, so the hardware doesn't know to pop the ExceptionInfo the way it would if we were exiting a trap handler. I need to reconsider how the Yalnix hardware simulator actually works in this scenario.

Looking back at the documentation, it seems the key is that returning from a trap handler is what signals the hardware to pop ExceptionInfo and enter user mode. For KernelStart and the initial process, there must be a similar mechanism—either KernelStart itself is treated as a special trap handler return, or the process needs to be set up so it appears to return from a trap handler when it first runs.

When idle returns from KernelStart (via `return;`), it pops KernelStart's frame off the stack and returns to... the caller of KernelStart. In the initial boot, the caller of KernelStart is the hardware bootstrap code. The hardware is designed to set up the ExceptionInfo at the top of the kernel stack and then call KernelStart. When KernelStart returns, the bootstrap code knows to pop the ExceptionInfo and enter user mode.

Actually, let me look at this from a different angle. In a typical OS, new processes enter user mode by setting up a fake trap frame on the kernel stack and configuring the saved context so that when resumed, execution flows through the trap return mechanism. But on Yalnix, when idle resumes via ContextSwitch, it just returns to the middle of KernelStart as a normal function return—the hardware's trap return path never gets invoked because the hardware only handles that at the initial KernelStart call, not on subsequent context switches. Let me think about how Fork works:
1. Parent calls Fork (trap to kernel)
2. kern_fork creates child, copies address space, calls ContextSwitch(ForkSwitchFunc)
3. ForkSwitchFunc copies kernel stack from parent to child, saves context in both, returns parent's context
4. Parent continues from ContextSwitch → kern_fork → trap_kernel → returns to user mode
5. When child is scheduled, ContextSwitch resumes child at the same point after ForkSwitchFunc
6. Child continues from ContextSwitch → kern_fork → trap_kernel → returns to user mode

For the child, the return path is: ContextSwitch → kern_fork → trap_kernel → trap return → user mode. The child's kernel stack was copied from the parent, so it has the correct trap frame and call chain.

For idle, I need to manually construct the kernel stack so that when ContextSwitch resumes it, the return path flows through the trap mechanism just like a forked process. But actually, I'm overcomplicating this - the instructions say to build idle's initial state as if it's previously been in and out of the CPU, with just an infinite loop or Pause instruction. OK so the ttywrite test program does:
1. TtyWrite(0, "Hello world\n", 12);
2. Exit(0);

Simple. It writes "Hello world\n" to terminal 0 and exits.

The test checks TTYLOG.0 for "Hello world".

Now, the flow in the kernel:
1. Init (the ttywrite program) calls TtyWrite(0, "Hello world\n", 12)
2. Trap to kernel (TRAP_KERNEL, code = YALNIX_TTY_WRITE)3. trap_kernel calls kern_ttywrite(0, buf, 12, info)4. 

In kern_ttywrite, I check if the terminal is free, copy the message into the transmit buffer, mark the current process as blocked on TTY write, initiate the hardware transmission, and then yield to the scheduler so the idle process can run while the hardware does its work.

When the TTY transmit interrupt fires, the trap handler retrieves the completed write, unblocks the init process by moving it back to the ready queue, and clears the active write flag. A clock interrupt then triggers a context switch back to init, which resumes from the syscall and returns the byte count. Init then exits, and the output should appear in the log once the hardware finishes transmitting.

If the test fails, the culprit is likely one of these: TtyTransmit was never invoked or called with incorrect parameters, the TTY transmit interrupt never fired, the trap handler has a bug preventing proper unblocking, or init wasn't rescheduled. Since idle works for clock-based tests, the problem probably lies with TtyTransmit or TTY handling itself. I need to look more carefully at the TtyTransmit call and what arguments I'm passing—specifically whether the buffer I'm using is accessible to the hardware, since TtyTransmit reads from physical memory while my static array lives in Region 1.

The hardware should be able to access Region 1 since it's always mapped in kernel mode, and even if we context-switch to idle, Region 1 stays accessible. But I'm wondering if there's a timing issue with how the TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT interrupt is being delivered—it should work the same way as clock interrupts do.

blocks.text:
I see! The check functions look at the combined `output` which includes:- Yalnix stdout: printf/fprintf output from test programs
- TRACE file: TracePrintf output from kernel and user
- TTYLOG files: TtyWrite/TtyPrintf output

For check_ttywrite:```python
def check_ttywrite(output: str) -> Tuple[bool, str]:    if "Hello world" not in output:
        return (False, "Expected 'Hello world' in TtyWrite output")    return (True, "")
```

It checks if "Hello world" appears ANYWHERE in the combined output. 

The "Hello world" string gets written via TtyWrite to terminal 0, so it should show up in TTYLOG.0, but the test output shows that file is empty. This means TtyWrite output isn't making it to the terminal at all. The issue is likely that TtyTransmit is either being called with incorrect buffer contents or isn't being invoked properly in the first place.

Let me trace through the buffer handling in kern_ttywrite: I'm copying 12 bytes from the user-space pointer into tty_xmit_buf[0], then calling TtyTransmit with that buffer and length. The string "Hello world\n" is indeed 12 bytes, so the buffer setup looks correct.

Now checking the function arguments themselves—TtyTransmit(0, tty_xmit_buf[0], 12) has the right tty_id, a valid kernel buffer pointer, and the correct length. But I should verify what happens when the user initially calls TtyWrite with those parameters. Now I'm considering whether TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT is actually being triggered at all. Clock interrupts work since the Delay tests pass, so the interrupt delivery mechanism itself is functioning. But maybe there's something about TTY interrupts specifically that needs additional setup, or perhaps the test runner is interfering by capturing the TTY output somehow.

Looking at the exit code 124 from the test output—that's a timeout kill, which means the kernel is hanging even after calling TtyTransmit. This suggests the interrupt isn't firing at all, despite the clock interrupt mechanism working fine. I'm wondering if maybe TtyTransmit requires the terminal to be in a specific state first, like needing a prior TtyReceive call or some other initialization step I'm overlooking.

Let me dig into the hardware manual section on terminals to understand the exact requirements. The key constraint I'm seeing is that the kernel can't touch the buffer until after TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT fires, but I need to understand what conditions must be met before TtyTransmit will actually trigger that interrupt.

Wait—there's a critical issue here. If TtyTransmit generates the interrupt while the kernel is still running (say, during the schedule() call), and the hardware doesn't queue interrupts in kernel mode, that interrupt could be lost entirely. The manual says interrupts aren't delivered while in kernel mode, but it's ambiguous whether they're queued for later or simply discarded. If they're lost, then calling TtyTransmit from within the kernel creates a race condition where the interrupt might fire and disappear before the kernel ever returns to user mode to receive it.

Let me check the Yalnix hardware specification again to clarify how interrupts are actually handled. When the Yalnix machine is running in kernel mode (when the PSR_MODE bit of the PSR is set), the hardware does NOT interrupt the processor for any interrupt. This doesn't necessarily mean the interrupt is lost—it might be pending and delivered once the processor returns to user mode.

If that's the case, then when TtyTransmit completes while we're still in kernel mode, the interrupt would be queued and fire as soon as idle enters user mode, unblocking the writer and allowing it to resume. But the test is timing out, which suggests interrupts might actually be getting lost instead.

Let me think about how Delay works differently. When kern_delay blocks a process, we schedule to idle, which enters user mode, and then the clock interrupt fires while idle is running in user mode—no lost interrupt there. But with TtyTransmit, the transmit could complete before we even context switch to idle, meaning the TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT fires during kernel mode and gets lost. In the Yalnix simulator, TtyTransmit starts an asynchronous operation that completes after some simulated time proportional to the character count. If that takes at least one clock tick, the interrupt fires during idle's user mode. But if the transmit is instantaneous, the interrupt fires immediately in kernel mode and might be lost.

Maybe I should try a different approach: instead of blocking immediately after TtyTransmit, I could set up the write and return to user mode, letting the TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT fire when the transmit completes and handling it in the trap handler. But TtyWrite is supposed to be blocking—it shouldn't return until the write is complete.

What if I defer the TtyTransmit call itself? I could have kern_ttywrite save the write request and block the process, then call TtyTransmit later from the clock tick handler. This way the transmit starts from the clock trap handler, and the TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT fires during idle's user mode afterward, avoiding the race condition where the interrupt fires in kernel mode.

But this adds at least one clock tick of latency before transmission starts. A simpler approach might be to call TtyTransmit after schedule returns in kern_ttywrite—that way the process is already blocked and scheduled before the transmit begins.

Actually, I think deferring the transmit to the clock handler is cleaner. I'd mark a pending flag for each terminal and let the clock interrupt handle the actual transmission.

Setting up deferred transmit...

But wait, there's a timing issue here—if the clock handler fires while we're still in kernel mode, the subsequent TTY transmit trap might get lost since interrupts are disabled. Let me think through whether that's actually a problem in this context.

The transmit operation is asynchronous in the simulator, so it fires at a later simulated time during user mode execution rather than immediately. That should mean the trap fires safely in user mode. Though there's still a risk if the next clock tick runs before the transmit interrupt gets delivered. I think I should just try implementing the deferred approach and see if it works.

Actually, let me reconsider whether the real problem is something else entirely. Looking at the kern_ttywrite function, it blocks the process, calls TtyTransmit, then schedules. When the process resumes and kern_ttywrite returns, I need to check what state current_proc is in at that point.

The process gets unblocked by trap_tty_transmit through enqueue_ready, then switched back in by the scheduler. So when it resumes, current_proc should be PROC_RUNNING. And I see that trap_tty_transmit correctly clears tty_write_active[tty_id] to NULL after unblocking the writer. The function returns len (12), which trap_kernel then needs to handle properly.

But here's the key insight: the test times out with exit code 124, which means the program never calls Exit(0). That only happens if TtyWrite never returns, which means the process is stuck waiting in the kernel—specifically stuck waiting for TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT that never arrives.

Let me explore a different angle: what if the interrupt fires during the context switch itself, before the idle process even starts running? I'm thinking through the exact sequence of events when kern_ttywrite initiates the hardware transmit, blocks the process, and calls schedule. If TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT arrives at just the wrong moment—after the process is marked blocked but before the idle context is fully restored—the interrupt handler might not find the right process to unblock, leaving everything deadlocked. I'm wondering if TtyTransmit might be doing something unexpected—maybe it's not actually triggering the interrupt at all, or there's some other mechanism at play that I'm missing.

Actually, I just realized another possibility: what if TtyTransmit is a blocking call in the simulator? If it waits for the transmit to complete before returning, calling it from kernel mode would deadlock since interrupts don't fire in kernel mode. The documentation says it should be asynchronous, but there could be a bug in the simulator.

Let me test the deferred approach—instead of calling TtyTransmit directly, I'll schedule it to happen on the next clock tick after the process blocks. If that fixes it, I'll know there's a timing issue with calling TtyTransmit in the same kernel context as the scheduler.

Actually, let me try something simpler first. What if I call TtyTransmit after switching to idle? The sequence would be: block the process, schedule and context switch to idle, then when the clock fires, call TtyTransmit from the trap handler before returning to user mode. This way the transmit happens outside the original kernel context. I'd need a flag to track when there's a pending write, but it's cleaner than deferring to a later tick.

Calling TtyTransmit from the trap handler itself...

hmm, that doesn't really solve the problem. Let me just go with the deferred approach.

Wait, I think I found the actual issue. What if the problem is that I'm marking the process as blocked before calling TtyTransmit, and then schedule isn't handling the blocked state correctly? Let me trace through the logic: I set the process state to blocked, call TtyTransmit, then call schedule. In schedule, I dequeue the next ready process, but if there's no ready process and the current one is blocked, I need to handle that case properly.

So when the TTY transmit trap fires later, it finds the blocked process in tty_write_active, enqueues it back to ready, and sets its state to ready. That should work, but I need to make sure the process actually gets woken up and scheduled correctly.

Looking at the ttywrite test output, I'm only seeing the boot messages and then a timeout—no debug output from my fprintf calls, which is strange. The simulator does produce output for other traps like illegal instructions, so something's preventing my kernel code from running or producing output in this specific test case. The output message format doesn't match what I wrote in my trap_illegal function, so this error message must be coming from the simulator itself rather than my code. Let me trace through my actual trap handler to figure out what's happening.

Looking at my trap_memory handler, I can see it uses fprintf to write "Memory fault in process %d at PC %p: No mapping at 0x%lx." — and that matches exactly with the test output showing "Memory fault in process 1 at PC 0x10169: No mapping at 0x123." So the stderr output is definitely coming from my handler code.

But for trap_illegal, the test shows "Illegal instruction fault in process 1 at PC 0x10160: Illegal instruction." while my handler just prints "Process %d terminated: illegal instruction" — these don't match at all. Either the simulator itself is generating that message when it detects the illegal instruction, or my fprintf output appears separately after the simulator's message.

The key insight is that fprintf(stderr, ...) definitely works in the kernel since it produced output for test_trap_illegal. So I should add debug statements to the TTY write path to see what's actually happening. But here's the puzzle: the TRACE shows "YalnixTtyWrite: pid 1" which means trap_kernel received the call, yet if kern_ttywrite were executing and printing debug output, it would show up in the Yalnix output — but it doesn't. So either kern_ttywrite isn't being called at all, or something else is preventing the output from appearing. I'm going to try a different approach instead of continuing to debug—let me implement the deferred TtyTransmit strategy where I call it from a clock handler after the kernel returns to user mode, ensuring the TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT interrupt fires reliably. But first, I should check if TtyTransmit is actually a blocking call that never returns, which would explain why the test hangs. from kernel mode (where interrupts are disabled) would deadlock, because the transmit completion relies on an interrupt that can't be delivered in kernel mode. Let me check: the TtyTransmit documentation says "On a TtyTransmit call, the hardware will begin writing..." — "begin" implies it's asynchronous and returns immediately. But what if the simulator implementation blocks?

If TtyTransmit blocks, then kern_ttywrite calls TtyTransmit, the simulator starts writing to TTYLOG.0, but then waits for the write to complete. The completion requires delivering TRAP_TTY_TRANSMIT, which can't happen in kernel mode — deadlock.

But wait, TTYLOG.0 is completely empty in the test output. If TtyTransmit blocked while writing, at least some characters would appear. Unless TtyTransmit blocks before writing anything, just setting up the hardware without actually starting until the interrupt can be delivered. That doesn't quite add up either.

Let me reconsider — maybe the problem is that I'm calling TtyTransmit with the wrong buffer. I'm using a static transmit buffer array that's allocated in the BSS segment, and I'm wondering if there's an issue with how that buffer is being passed or accessed. I'm going to try a simpler debugging approach first—adding fprintf statements to kern_ttywrite to see if it's actually being called, since the TracePrintf output might be misleading or truncated. I'll also instrument trap_kernel to trace the control flow and confirm whether the YALNIX_TTY_WRITE trap is reaching the right handler.

Key insight: `test_brk_delay` passes, which confirms idle works correctly. The issue must be specifically with TtyTransmit/TtyWrite. Let me add `fprintf(stderr, ...)` debug output that will appear in the non-truncated Yalnix output:

Interesting! My fprintf(stderr, "DBG: ...") messages DON'T appear in the Yalnix output for the ttywrite test:

```
Yalnix output: Booting...
** Kernel   level 0 tracing to file 'TRACE' **
** User     level 0 tracing to file 'TRACE' **
    Yalnix exit code: 124
```

No "DBG: TTY_WRITE" or "DBG: IDLE RESUMED" messages! This is very strange. It means either:
1. The fprintf(stderr, ...) calls are not being executed


2. The Yalnix output is captured differently than I thought
3. stderr output in the kernel doesn't go to the Yalnix output

But wait, for test_trap_memory:
```
Yalnix output: Booting...
** Kernel   level 0 tracing to file 'TRACE' **
** User     level 0 tracing to file 'TRACE' **
Memory fault in process 1 at PC 0x10169: No mapping at 0x123.
```

The "Memory fault in process 1..." message IS appearing, which means my fprintf(stderr, ...) calls in trap_memory are working. So the issue must be specific to the ttywrite test—either those code paths aren't being hit, or something about how that particular output is being handled is different.

The TRACE file shows "YalnixTtyWrite: pid 1", confirming the YALNIX_TTY_WRITE case is entered, but my fprintf(stderr, ...) in that same case isn't showing up in the output. Both TracePrintf and fprintf should execute if the case is reached, unless the TRACE and Yalnix output are being captured or routed differently than I'm assuming.

Looking at the test output more carefully, the "Actual" field combines multiple sources—Yalnix stdout, TRACE, and TTYLOG—before passing to the check functions. The combined output includes content from all three, but my stderr fprintf still isn't appearing anywhere. in the "Actual" field. The "Actual" field shows:
- "TLB before read()" — from TRACE
- "Doing read:" — from TRACE  
- "Bottom of KernelStart:" — from TRACE
- "YalnixTtyWrite: pid 1" — from TRACE
- "Bottom of " — truncated from TRACE

So the "Actual" field only shows TRACE content. The Yalnix output field shows:
```
Booting...
** Kernel   level 0 tracing to file 'TRACE' **
** User     level 0 tracing to file 'TRACE' **
    Yalnix exit code: 124
```

No fprintf(stderr) output! This means either:
1. fprintf(stderr) doesn't work in the kernel for this test
2. The code path isn't reached
3. The output is captured differently

But for test_trap_memory, the Yalnix output shows a memory fault message that exactly matches my fprintf(stderr) format in trap_memory, so stderr definitely works there. Yet my fprintf(stderr) in trap_kernel for the ttywrite test produces nothing — this is puzzling, and it might be a buffering issue even though stderr should be unbuffered.

The fprintf(stderr) debug messages don't appear for the ttywrite test. This might be because the test is killed by timeout, and the output buffer isn't flushed. Let me check how the test runner captures output:

blocks.text:
Let me continue working on fixing the 3 failing TTY write tests. Based on the summary, the key insight is that:

1. The idle process IS working correctly (brk_delay test passes)
2. The fprintf(stderr) debug messages don't appear for TTY write tests
3. This suggests TtyTransmit might be blocking or the interrupt is lost

The suggested next step is to try a "deferred TtyTransmit" approach. But first, let me re-read the current state of yalnix.c to understand exactly what we're working with, and then think more carefully about what might be going wrong.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. 

The pattern is clear: brk_delay passes which means idle works and clock interrupts fire, but ttywrite times out and the debug output never shows up. So when the init process calls TtyWrite, it traps into the kernel, calls TtyTransmit to send the data, blocks itself, and schedules the idle process—but something's preventing the idle process from running or the interrupt from firing to wake things back up.

The missing fprintf suggests the trap isn't even reaching the YALNIX_TTY_WRITE handler, which points to either a syscall number mismatch or the trap dispatch logic itself being broken.

I need to verify the register mapping for syscall arguments—the convention should have the syscall number in info->code and the three arguments in specific registers, but I should double-check the actual hardware.h documentation to confirm which registers are being used.

Now I'm thinking through the schedule function and whether it properly handles the case where the only runnable process blocks—it needs to switch to the idle process. I'm also considering whether context is being saved and restored correctly when kern_ttywrite calls schedule, since the process needs to resume from that point when unblocked and return the result to the caller.

Let me look at the actual code to trace through what happens: when kern_ttywrite blocks and schedule switches contexts, the process eventually gets switched back in, returns from the context switch, and then returns from kern_ttywrite to trap_kernel, which sets the return value. The question is whether the ExceptionInfo structure on the kernel stack is still valid at that point.

I'm also reconsidering the deferred TtyTransmit approach—maybe the issue is that TtyTransmit needs to be non-blocking and return immediately while the hardware handles transmission asynchronously, then the TRAP_TTY_TRANSM interrupt fires when transmission completes. I'm realizing there's a critical issue with how the idle process's execution context gets set up. When we context-switch to save idle's state and then return, the hardware jumps to user mode using the ExceptionInfo structure. But that same ExceptionInfo gets overwritten during init process setup, so idle would actually end up executing init's code instead of its own infinite loop. The idle process is getting its execution context corrupted by the initialization of the next process.

Actually, wait—I need to reconsider how the kernel stacks work here. Each process should have its own kernel stack, so when idle resumes via context switch, it should be using its own stack with its own ExceptionInfo intact. But the problem is that initially there's only one kernel stack provided by the hardware, and both processes share it until we set up separate stacks for each one.

The real question is whether the ContextSwitch properly saves and restores the kernel stack pointer so that when idle resumes later, it's back on the right stack with the right ExceptionInfo. Let me look at the actual code to see if there's a subtle bug in how this is being handled.
Continuing to debug the 3 failing TTY write tests. Let me re-read the current state of the kernel code, focusing on the key areas: `schedule`, `kern_ttywrite`, and the idle process setup.
